questions.question: According to research, what percentage of mentors/wisdom-sharers provide some services free while monetizing others?

options.text: About 25% use a mixed model
About 45% use a mixed model
About 67% use a mixed model (some free, some paid)
Nearly all (95%+) either charge for everything or nothing—mixed models are rare

questions.explanation: Research suggests approximately 67% of mentors provide some services free while monetizing others. This 'mixed model' recognizes that some forms of contribution should remain gifts (building community, serving those in need, paying it forward) while others require compensation for sustainability. Most people find a balance between free and paid offerings rather than all-or-nothing approaches.

questions.question: What does research show about diversified income streams compared to single-source income?

options.text: Single-source income is more sustainable and focused
Diversified income streams show 2.8x greater sustainability
There's no difference in sustainability between models
Diversified streams are too complicated to maintain

questions.explanation: Research shows approximately 2.8x greater sustainability with diversified income streams (courses + coaching + workshops + digital products) compared to single-source models. When one revenue stream dries up, others continue—providing resilience and stability. Relying on a single income source (e.g., only 1-on-1 coaching) creates vulnerability when that source changes or disappears.

questions.question: What is the median rate for professional mentoring/coaching services according to the lesson?

options.text: About $50/hour
About $75/hour
About $125/hour
About $250/hour

questions.explanation: The lesson cites median rates for professional mentoring/coaching at approximately $125/hour, with a typical range of $75-300/hour depending on experience and specialization. However, these are baseline market rates that should be adjusted for: your experience level, audience capacity, local economics, and value provided. The number is a starting reference point, not a prescription—your actual pricing should fit your circumstances and context.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what is the healthiest stance regarding motivation for paid wisdom-sharing work?

options.text: Service should be your only motivation—money shouldn't matter at all
Income should be your primary motivation—it's a business transaction
Balanced: wanting to serve well AND be fairly compensated, with both mattering
Never think about money—it corrupts authentic wisdom-sharing

questions.explanation: The healthiest stance is balanced: wanting to serve well AND be fairly compensated, with both mattering. This is realistic, sustainable long-term, maintains healthy boundaries, and allows you to say no to poor fits. Pure service motivation (money doesn't matter) risks financial strain and resentment. Pure income motivation (business first) may compromise quality and authentic voice. Most people need 1-2 years to find this balance through practice.

questions.question: When does relationship with money in wisdom-sharing become unhealthy?

options.text: Whenever you charge anything for your time and expertise
When you make decisions primarily for profit, exploit vulnerability, overpromise results, or keep prices so low you resent the work
When you use sliding scale pricing or offer scholarships
When you have diversified income streams

questions.explanation: Unhealthy money relationships show up as: making decisions primarily for profit over service, feeling ashamed or defensive about charging, exploiting vulnerable people's desperation, overpromising results to justify high prices, or keeping prices so low you resent the work. Healthy balance means fair compensation for valuable service without exploitation or martyr sacrifice. Charging for services, using sliding scales, offering scholarships, and diversifying income are all healthy practices when done ethically.

title: Financial Sustainability in Legacy Work - Quiz
